coredata.dc:title: Dual nanoenzyme modified microelectrode based on carbon fiber coated with AuPd alloy nanoparticles decorated graphene quantum dots assembly for electrochemical detection in clinic cancer samples
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Qi
Yuan, Hao
Dong, Xulin
Zhang, Yan
Asif, Muhammad
Dong, Zehua
He, Wenshan
Ren, Jinghua
Sun, Yimin
Xiao, Fei
coredata.dc:description: The development of high-efficient technologies for cancer biomarkers detection has attracted tremendous research effort for its great clinic significance. In this work, we designed a new type of flexible and robust nanohybrid microelectrode by modifying carbon fiber with dual nanoenzyme, i.e., AuPd alloy nanoparticles (AuPd-ANPs) decorated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) assembly, and explored its practical application in electrochemical sensing system for sensitive detection of cancer biomarker hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in human breast cancer cells and tissue. For the preparation of dual nanoenzyme modified microelectrode, ionic liquid was used as the electrolyte for the effective electrodeposition of GQDs on carbon fiber substrate to form a close-packed assembly under a very negative potential, then the highly dense AuPd-ANPs were uniformly decorated on GQDs assembly by electrodeposition. In virtue of the structural merits and synergistic contribution of dual nanoenzyme in enhancing the electrocatalytic activity to H2O2, the resultant nanohybrid microelectrode exhibited good sensing performances for electrochemical detection of H2O2, including a high sensitivity of 371 μA cm−2 mM−1, a wide linear range from 1.0 μM to 18.44 mM, a low detection limit of 500 nM (a signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1), as well as good selectivity and biocompatibility, which could be used for real-time tracking H2O2 released from different types of human breast cells and in situ sensitive detection of H2O2 in clinical breast cancer tissue.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots assembly
Alloy nanoparticles
Carbon fiber microelectrode
Electrochemical sensor
Cancer biomarker detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a)-(c) ACF, (d)-(f) GQDs/ACF and (g)-(i) AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF under different magnification, (j) Scanning elemental mapping of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF.

(a) Schematic illustration of the formation of GQDs assembly and AuPd-ANPs decorated GQDs assembly. Step i: Electrodeposition of GQDs on ACF using [BMIM]OTF as the electrolyte to form a close-pack GQDs assembly; Step ii: Electrodeposition of AuPd-ANPs on ACF on GQDs. (b) TEM image of GQDs layer peeled off from ACF substrate, inset of (a) is the digital photographs GQDs under sun and ultraviolet light and HR-TEM image of GQD. (c) HR-TEM image of GQDs assembly. (d) and (e) TEM images of integrated AuPd-ANPs/GQDs layer under different magnification. (f) and (g) HR-TEM images of AuPd-ANPs on GQDs assembly under different magnification.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF. Curve fit of (b) Au 4f, (c) Au 4d and Pd 3d, (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s, (f) N 1s, (g) S 2p and (h) F 1 s.

(a) CV curves of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF, Au-NPs/GQDs/ACF and Pd-NPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrodes in 0.5 M H2SO4. Scan rate: 50 mV s−1. (b) CV curves of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF, AuPd-ANPs/ACF and GQDs/ACF microelectrodes in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 5 mM H2O2. Scan rate: 50 mV s−1. (c) CV curves of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF, Pd-NPs/GQDs/ACF and Au-NPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrodes in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 5 mM H2O2. Scan rate: 50 mV s−1. (d) CV curves of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF electrode in 0.1 M PBS solution containing 0, 2, 5 and 10 mM H2O2. Scan rate: 50 mV s−1. (e) Typical amperometric response of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode to successive addition of different H2O2 concentration into 0.1 M PBS solution under stirring. Inset of (e) is the amperometric response of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF electrode at low concentrations. Applied potential: 0.05 V. (f) Amperometric response of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF to successive addition of 0.1 mM H2O2 and 0.1 mM UA, GLU, AA and DA in 0.1 M PBS. Applied potential: 0.05 V.

Fluorescent images of (a) MCF-7 cells, (b) MDA-MB-231 cells and (c) HBL-100 cells by calcein AM assay. Quantitative cell viability results by CCK-8 assay for (d) MCF-7, (e) MDA-MB-231 and (f) HBL-100 cells incubated with AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode for 0 h, 1 h, 2 h, 3 h, 4 h and 5 h. (g) Amperometric current responses of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode to the addition of 50 µM fMLP and 500 U mL−1 catalase in test solution containing 5.0 × 106 MCF-7 cells and in the absence of cells. (h) Amperometric current responses of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode to the addition of 50 µM fMLP in test solution containing 5.0 × 106 MCF-7, MDA-MB-231 and HBL-100 cells. (i) Histograms of the relative increased amperometric current densities related to H2O2 secreted from MCF-7, MDA-MB-231 and HBL-100 cells.

Digital photographs of (a) the surgically resected clinical breast specimen from a female patient with primary breast cancer, (b) the breast cancer tissue obtained by detaching the enclosed adipose tissue, and (c) the three-electrode setup for tissue measurement, with AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode as WE, Ag/AgCl as RE and Pt wire as CE, the flexible AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode was wound into a helical shape and inserting inside the breast cancer tissue. (d) Amperometric current response of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode to the addition of 50 µM fMLP and 500 U mL−1 catalase in the breast cancer tissue sample soaked in PBS solution.

head.ce:title: Dual nanoenzyme modified microelectrode based on carbon fiber coated with AuPd alloy nanoparticles decorated graphene quantum dots assembly for electrochemical detection in clinic cancer samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key laboratory of Material Chemistry for Energy Conversion and Storage, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Material Chemistry and Service Failure, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key laboratory of Material Chemistry for Energy Conversion and Storage, Ministry of Education, Hubei Key Laboratory of Material Chemistry and Service Failure, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Union Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Union Hospital, Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Key Laboratory of Plasma Chemistry and Advanced Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology, Wuhan 430073, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Key Laboratory of Plasma Chemistry and Advanced Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Qi Xu and Hao Yuan contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of high-efficient technologies for cancer biomarkers detection has attracted tremendous research effort for its great clinic significance. In this work, we designed a new type of flexible and robust nanohybrid microelectrode by modifying carbon fiber with dual nanoenzyme, i.e., AuPd alloy nanoparticles (AuPd-ANPs) decorated graphene quantum dots (GQDs) assembly, and explored its practical application in electrochemical sensing system for sensitive detection of cancer biomarker hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) in human breast cancer cells and tissue. For the preparation of dual nanoenzyme modified microelectrode, ionic liquid was used as the electrolyte for the effective electrodeposition of GQDs on carbon fiber substrate to form a close-packed assembly under a very negative potential, then the highly dense AuPd-ANPs were uniformly decorated on GQDs assembly by electrodeposition. In virtue of the structural merits and synergistic contribution of dual nanoenzyme in enhancing the electrocatalytic activity to H2O2, the resultant nanohybrid microelectrode exhibited good sensing performances for electrochemical detection of H2O2, including a high sensitivity of 371 μA cm−2 mM−1, a wide linear range from 1.0 μM to 18.44 mM, a low detection limit of 500 nM (a signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1), as well as good selectivity and biocompatibility, which could be used for real-time tracking H2O2 released from different types of human breast cells and in situ sensitive detection of H2O2 in clinical breast cancer tissue.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid progress in identifying cancer biomarkers is of great significance in early cancer diagnosis, monitoring cancer recurrence, and evaluating the therapeutic treatment efficacy to cancer diseases (Kelley et al., 2014; Ludwig and Weinstein, 2005; Mishra and Verma, 2010). Among a number of advanced technologies for cancer biomarkers detection, electrochemical sensors have been touted as a particularly promising class of next-generation clinical diagnostics as they combine the compatible miniaturization of electrochemical instruments, and the key advantages of electroanalytical methods such as high precision and reliability, good selectivity and high sensitivity, and fast response (Bo et al., 2017; Kumar et al., 2018; Zhang and Wei, 2017a). Over the last decade, the design of electrochemical sensing platforms for cancer biomarkers detection has focused on developing high-performance biomacromolecule sensors such as nucleic acid sensors based on DNA and microRNAs to detect nucleic acid cancer biomarkers, and immunosensors to detect protein biomarkers such as α-fetoprotein, carcinoembryonic antigen, squamous cell carcinoma antigen, prostate-specific antigen, etc. (Ávila et al., 2015; Diamandis, 2010; Huo et al., 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Wan et al., 2013; Wang et al., 2017a). However, these methods require complicated and time-consuming biochemical preparation of target samples and chemical-biological labeling steps (Drummond et al., 2003). As an alternative to macromolecule cancer biomarkers, small molecule metabolites in cellular signaling and homeostasis process, e.g., hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), which forms by disproportionation of unstable reactive oxygen species (ROS), has been recognized as a new type of biomarker for early-stage recognition of cancer (Xiao et al., 2016), since mounting evidences reveal that living cancer cells release more H2O2 than their normal counterparts due to the higher ROS production or lower ROS scavenging capacities for the abnormal growth of tumors (Xi et al., 2016; Xiao et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2017b). As a result, the sensitive detection of H2O2 level in living cells and tissues is of vital importance in early-stage diagnosis of cancer diseases.

Despite recent impressive progress in the field of electrochemical H2O2 sensors based on enzyme (e.g., horseradish peroxide), they suffer from several shortcomings such as easy denaturation by environmental changes, deficient long-term stability and high cost (Kuila et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2017b). With the prompt development of nanotechnology and biotechnology, the nanomaterials based artificial enzymes, i.e., nanozymes, have been developed as well (Wang et al., 2016b; Zhou et al., 2017). Several researches have shown that some metal (oxide) nanomaterials such as Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticles (Jiang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2008), Co3O4 nanoparticles (Mu et al., 2012), CuO nanoparticles (Hu et al., 2017), Au nanoparticles (Wang et al., 2012; Zheng et al., 2011), Au clusters (Wang et al., 2011), Pt nanoparticles (Fan et al., 2011; Zhang et al., 2010), Au@M (M=Bi, Pd and Pt) (Lien et al., 2012), AgM (M=Au, Pd, Pt) nanoalloys (He et al., 2010), and carbon based nanomaterials such as carbon nanoparticles (Dong et al., 2012a; Li et al., 2013), carbon nanotubes (Cui et al., 2011), C60 (Li et al., 2013) and graphene oxide (Song et al., 2010), exhibit intrinsic peroxidase-like activity by mimicking the structures and functions of natural enzymes, and have been employed in colorimetric, fluorometric, chemiluminescent and electrochemical sensing systems to detect H2O2. Especially, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a zero-dimensional graphene material with lateral sizes less than 100 nm, have drawn great attention for their unique optical and electronic properties (Tang et al., 2012; Yan et al., 2013). GQDs possess peroxidase mimetic activity originated from their intact aromatic structure and rich periphery carboxylic groups, and large surface area owing to their quantum confinement and edge effects (Lin et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2012; Wei and Wang, 2013), which have exhibited enhanced electrocatalytic activity to H2O2 reaction (Song and Chen, 2014). In view of the unique catalytic activity, the large specific surface area, as well as the good biocompatibility of nanoenzyme, the employment of the nanoenzyme-based peroxidase mimetics in electrochemical H2O2 sensing system is appreciated to improve its sensitivity and selectivity.

In this work, we develop a dual nanoenzyme modified microelectrode by coating carbon fiber (CF) with AuPd alloy nanoparticles (AuPd-ANPs) decorated GQDs assembly. CF based flexible microelectrodes have demonstrated great capability for real-time and in situ monitoring biological signals owing to their high temporal and spatial resolution for fast electron and mass transfer as well as their excellent mechanical strength and flexibility (Shi et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2016a; Zhang et al., 2017a). To fabricate dual nanoenzyme modified CF microelectrode, CF was firstly activated and coated by GQDs layer via electrodepositing GQDs on activated CF (ACF) using ionic liquid 1-butyl-3-methylimidazolium trifluoromethanesulfonate ([BMIM]OTF, Fig. S1) as the electrolyte, which contributes to a highly efficient electrodeposition of GQDs on ACF at a very negative potential of −3 V on account of the characteristic physicochemical properties of [BMIM]OTF. During this process, GQDs have assembled into a close-packed structure, and [BMIM]OTF molecules adsorb onto GQDs via cation–π interaction between the imidazolium ring moiety of [BMIM]OTF and π–electronic surface of GQDs. In order to enhance the electrocatalytic activity, another type of nanoenzyme, AuPd-ANPs have been decorated on GQDs assembly by electrochemical co-deposition of Au and Pd from their precursor solutions. The [BMIM]OTF molecules grafted on GQDs provide sufficient active sites for the nucleation of AuPd-ANPs and increase their nucleation rate as well due to the low interfacial tension of ionic liquid, and GQDs possess large specific surface area and controlled effect on the size of AuPd-ANPs by their quantum confinement and edge effects, these facilitate the formation of high-density ultrafine AuPd-ANPs on GQDs assembly.

Benefiting from the dual nanoenzymatic activity of AuPd-ANPs and GQDs assembly, the ACF modified by AuPd-ANPs decorated GQDs assembly (AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF) possesses excellent electrocatalytic properties to H2O2. Under the optimized condition, the electrochemical H2O2 sensor based on AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode exhibits a variety of outstanding features, including a high sensitivity of 371 μA cm−2 mM−1, a wide linear range from 1.0 μM to18.44 mM, a low detection limit of 500 nM (a signal-to-noise ratio S/N = 3), as well as good selectivity and biocompatibility. For real-time tracking H2O2 secreted from living human breast cancer cells, the AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode can distinguish different types of human breast cells by detecting the released amount of H2O2 varied from these cells. Furthermore, for in situ detecting H2O2 in clinical breast cancer tissue, the AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode inserting inside the tissue can measure trace amount of H2O2 secreted from living cancer cells in tissue. Therefore, the electrochemical sensing platform based on AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode holds great promise for in-vitro and in-vivo detection of cancer biomarker in different clinical specimens for real application.

CFs (diameter of ~10 µm and length of ~2.5 cm) were purchased from Fuel Cell Earth, USA. Before electrodeposition, a bundle of CFs was ultrasonically washed by acetone, alcohol, and distilled water for several times. After that, the CFs were immersed in a mixed solution of H2O2 and alcohol (volume ratio 1:1, without any purification) for 48 h in a 60 °C oven. The as-obtained activated CFs (ACFs) were cleaned with distilled water and freeze-dried.

The GQDs were synthesized based on a bottom-up method (Dong et al., 2012b), which were dried at a room temperature and dispersed in [BMIM]OTF to form a golden suspension (10 mg mL−1) by sonication for 30 min. In order to remove the remaining water, the suspension was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 30 °C for 48 h. The electrodeposition of GQDs on ACF was conducted by a standard three-electrode system in [BMIM]OTF electrolyte containing GQDs at a constant potential of −3 V for 10 min, using ACF as the working electrode (WE), Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode (RE) and Pt wire as the counter electrode (CE). The as-prepared GQDs assembly modified ACF (GQDs/ACF) was carefully washed with water for several times to remove the physically adsorbing [BMIM]OTF molecules on it. The further electrodeposition of AuPd-ANPs on GQDs/ACF was carried out in 0.1 M NaNO3 aqueous solution containing 1.5 mM HAuCl4 and 0.3 mM K2PdCl4 under a constant voltage of − 0.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 60 s. For comparison, Au-NPs/GQDs/ACF and Pd-NPs/GQDs/ACF were prepared under the same experimental steps. AuPd-ANPs/ACF was synthesized by directly electrodepositing AuPd-ANPs on ACF. All of the modified microelectrodes were washed by distilled water and freeze-dried.

The human living cells were purchased from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). The cell culture process was performed in appropriate medium containing 10% fetal calf serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin streptomycin (10,000 IU penicillin and 10,000 μg/mL streptomycin, Multicell) in a culture dish at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2. The media was Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM, Multicell) for all living cells.

The clinical specimen of human primary breast tumor was received within two hours after surgery at Union Hospital of Huazhong University of Science & Technology (HUST) in February 2017, from a 35-year-old female patient with primary breast cancer. The surgically resected specimen was a breast cancer tissue enclosed within adipose tissue. By carefully dissecting the adipose tissue, the breast cancer tissue was obtained, which was washed with PBS solution for several times and soaked in PBS solution at 37 ℃ for electrochemical testing.

Fig. 1a inset shows the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of pristine CF, which demonstrates that the bare CF, with a typical diameter of ~10 µm, is a bendable and tough material that can act as an ideal flexible microelectrode substrate. For the fabrication of dual nanoenzyme modified CF, the CFs were chemically activated to increase their hydrophilicity and surface rough (Fig. 1b and c), which facilitated the electrodeposition of GQDs on their surface. Subsequently, the electrodeposition of GQDs on ACF was performed using ACF as the working electrode in [BMIM]OTF electrolyte containing GQDs at a very negative potential of −3 V. During this process, the ACF was completely wrapped up with uniform GQDs layer (Fig. 1d–f), and [BMIM]OTF molecules have chemically absorbed on GQDs surface by cation–π interaction. The characterization of different microelectrodes is also manifested by Raman spectrum (Fig. S2), showing an in-phase vibration of graphite lattice at 1594 cm−1 (G band) and 1350 cm−1 (D band) for ACF. Similarly, the spectrum of GQDs/ACF also exhibits the presence of D band and G band at 1348 and 1599 cm−1, respectively. However, the D/G intensity ratio of GQDs/ACF increases in comparison to ACF, which is due to the decrease in the size of in-plane sp2 domains and the defect structure of GQDs wrapped on ACF.

The modification of ACF by GQDs not only improves its electrocatalytic activity but also endows it with larger surface area and plenty active sites for the nucleation of metal nanoparticles. To prepare AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode, AuPd-ANPs were grown on the surface of GQDs/ACF by electrodeposition, which exhibits ultrahigh density and uniform particle size distribution (Fig. 1g–i). However, AuPd-ANPs deposited on bare ACF under the same experimental procedure display a more sparse and inhomogenous distribution with large size range (Fig. S3). Significantly, GQDs grafted with [BMIM]OTF molecules play an important role in the nucleation and growth of AuPd-ANPs. This is because GQDs possess large specific surface area and controlled effect on the size of AuPd-ANPs by their quantum confinement and edge effects, AuPd-ANPs can be stabilized in GQDs matrix as the nucleation of AuPd-ANPs occurs at the dense edge-plane-like defects on GQDs (Ge et al., 2012; Jurkschat et al., 2006). Meanwhile, the [BMIM]OTF molecules grafted on GQDs provide sufficient active sites for the nucleation of AuPd-ANPs and increase their nucleation rate as well due to the low interfacial tension of ionic liquid (Chen and Dong, 2007; Xiao et al., 2009). These facilitate the deposition of high-density ultrafine AuPd-ANPs on GQD layer. The SEM mapping further verifies the uniform and intense elemental distribution including C, O, N, S, F, Au and Pd in the AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode (Fig. 1j), which testifies the presence of GQDs, [BMIM]OTF molecules and AuPd-ANPs in the nanohybrid microelectrode.

Fig. 2a illustrates the formation of GQD assembly and AuPd-ANPs anchored on GQD assembly. Their nanostructure and crystal structure are also investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization. As shown in Fig. 2b inset, the GQDs synthesized by bottom-up method exhibit yellow light under sun and blue phosphors under ultraviolet light. The high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of GQD shows a spacing of lattice fringe of 0.34 nm, consistent with the d-spacing of (002) lattice fringe of graphene crystalline structure (Tang et al., 2012). During the electrodeposition process using ionic liquid [BMIM]OTF under the very negative potential of −3 V, the GQDs deposited on ACF have assembled into a close-packed film (Fig. 2b). From the HR-TEM image, it can be observed that the close-packed film is composed of interconnect GQDs (Fig. 2c). The TEM images of AuPd-ANPs decorated GQDs assembly are shown in Fig. 2d and e, revealing that numerous AuPd-ANPs are uniformly anchored on the surface of GQDs assembly. The size distribution of AuPd-ANPs is in the range from 3 to 8 nm, with an average size of approximately 5.2 nm (Fig. S4), which has demonstrated anomalous reactivity towards electrocatalytic application in H2O2 sensing (Thanh et al., 2016). Both of AuPd-ANPs and GQDs support exhibit good crystallinity (Fig. 2f). The lattice finger of 0.34 nm assigned to graphene crystalline structure is evident for GQDs assembly. Moreover, the interplanar spacing for the lattice fingers of AuPd-ANP is measured to be 0.235 nm and 0.22 nm (Fig. 2g), which correspond to lattice planes of Au (111) and Pd (111), respectively (Ding et al., 2010), indicating the successful formation of AuPd alloy by atomic-level mixture of Au and Pd atoms, rather than a mixture of monometallic or core-shell Au and Pd NPs. The structural integration of AuPd-ANPs and GQDs assembly is anticipated to achieve a synergistic effect in enhancing the catalytic activity and stability in electrochemical reactions.

To further illustrate the formation of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs nanocomposite, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to characterize the surface composition and the electronic interactions of elements. Fig. 3a shows that the XPS spectrum of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs displays eight main peaks of Au 4f, Au 4d, Pd 3d, C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, S 2p and F 1s, which is in accordance with element composition of SEM mapping. The corresponding fitted curves of different elements have been demonstrated in Fig. 3b–h. The spectrum of Au shows double peaks assigning to Au 4 f7/2 and Au 4f5/2 at the binding energies of 84.33 and 87.97 eV, respectively (Fig. 3b). The overlapped Pd 3d and Au 4d spectra exhibit the distinct peaks at 335.4 and 340.6 eV corresponding to Pd 3d5/2 and Pd 3d3/2, respectively (Fig. 3c). And the peaks at the binding energies of 335.7 and 353.6 eV are attributed to Au 4d5/2 and Au 4d3/2, respectively. Compared to the standard values previously reported for Au 4d, Au 4f and Pd 3d (Lu et al., 2008; Zhang et al., 2012), the binding energies in Au 4f and Au 4d concurrently shift to higher values, while the peak of Pd 3d negatively shifts by ~0.5 eV. This is due to the fact that the formation of AuPd alloy gives rise to the tendency for Au to lose electrons so that the migration of electrons from Au sites to Pd sites occurs during the co-deposition process (Sarina et al., 2013; Xiao et al., 2014). The surface atom ratio of Au/Pd is analyzed by the peak area ratio of the XPS spectrum, which is 6:1 for Au/Pd in AuPd-ANPs. Furthermore, the C 1s spectrum deconvolutes four peaks at 284.9 eV, 285.7 eV, 287.2 eV and 288.5 eV assigned to C˭C/C–C, C–N, C–O and C˭O bonds, respectively (Fig. 3d). And the O 1s spectrum exhibits the presence of several oxygen-containing groups, including C˭O bond at 531.6 eV, O–C˭O bond at 532.9 eV, and C–O bond at 534.4 eV, respectively (Fig. 3e). Additionally, the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s, S 2p and F 1s are presented in Fig. 3f–h. These are originated from GQDs and [BMIM]OTF molecules grafted on their surface.

The electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of different microelectrodes has been evaluated by cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements using [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- as the redox probe (Fig. S5). According to the Randles–Sevcik equation (Lu et al., 2008), the ECSA values of the different electrodes are calculated to be 0.53 cm2, 0.68 cm2 and 0.95 cm2 for bare ACF, GQDs/ACF and AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrodes, respectively. Benefiting from the large specific surface area of GQDs assembly and high loading of AuPd-ANPs on it, the resultant AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode presents largest ECSA value among all the modified microelectrodes. Furthermore, the electrochemical activity of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode has also been investigated by CV measurement in deaerated 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, with Au-NPs/GQDs/ACF and Pd-NPs/GQDs/ACF as the control microelectrodes. As shown in Fig. 4a, all of the nanohybrid microelectrodes exhibit typical oxide formation/reduction peaks of polycrystalline bulk Au and/or Pd (Zhang et al., 2015). The peak at 0.87 V is associated with Au species of Au-NPs/GQDs/ACF, and the peak at 0.29 V is corresponding to Pd species of Pd-NPs/GQDs/ACF. For AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF, the prominent reduction peaks assigned to Au and Pd species can be observed at 0.86 and 0.14 V, respectively, confirming the effective co-deposition of Au and Pd to form AuPd-ANPs on the surface of GQDs/ACF.

The electrocatalytic activities of different microelectrodes to H2O2 redox reaction have been estimated by CV measurements in 0.1 M phosphate buffered saline (PBS) solution containing 5 mM H2O2 (Fig. 4b). The results show that there are no any noticeable redox peaks of H2O2 in CV curve of ACF since bare ACF possesses inappreciable electrocatalytic activity. For GQDs/ACF, a weak reduction peak can be observed at − 0.35 V, and the peak current densities slightly increase with the H2O2 concentration increasing from 0 to 10 mM (Fig. S6). Evidently, even though GQDs possess peroxidase mimetic activity and large surface area, their catalytic activity is still far from the requirement of sensitive electrochemical H2O2 sensor. In the case of AuPd-ANPs/ACF, the reduction peak current density of H2O2 increases by several times, and the peak potential shifts positively, indicating that the AuPd-ANPs play an important role in promoting the electron transfer in the redox reaction of H2O2 on electrode. For comparison, the reduction peak current density of H2O2 on AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF is much larger than that on AuPd-ANPs/ACF, and the peak potential shifts to 0.05 V. The improved electrocatalytic activity of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF should be attributed to the synergistic contribution of integrated dual nanoenzyme activity from AuPd-ANPs and GQDs assembly and the structural merits since the GQDs support has facilitated the nucleation and growth of AuPd-ANPs on it to form a high-loading and well-dispersed AuPd-ANPs, which significantly increase the catalytic activity of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode.

Furthermore, the CV curves of H2O2 on Au-NPs/GQDs/ACF and Pd-NPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrodes have been shown in Fig. 4c. Obviously, the reduction peak current density of H2O2 on AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode is much larger than that on Au-NPs/GQDs/ACF and Pd-NPs/GQDs/ACF. This is because bimetallic AuPd-ANPs on GQDs/ACF can substantially enhance the electrocatalytic activity on account of the electronic modification and bifunctional effects of two metallic components (Nantaphol et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2011). The CV curves of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF in PBS solution with different concentrations (i.e., 0, 2, 5, and 10 mM) of H2O2 are shown in Fig. 4d, which exhibit a sharp and linear increase of peak current densities with the H2O2 concentration increasing, revealing the excellent electrocatalytic properties of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode to H2O2 reduction.

The sensing performances of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode to H2O2 such as the linear range, detection limit and sensitivity have been investigated by amperometric measurement at the optimal potential of 0.05 V. Fig. 4e shows that upon the addition of different concentrations of H2O2 to PBS solution, well-defined, steady-state amperometric current response signals are collected within 4 s, and the current densities gradually increase with successive step increments of H2O2 concentration. The linear relationship between the corresponding current densities on AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode and the H2O2 concentrations demonstrates a wide linear dynamic range over the concentration of H2O2 from 1.0 μM to 18.44 mM (Fig. S7), and the regression equation is j (mA cm−2)= 0.371c (mM) + 0.157 (j: current density; c: concentration. The correlation coefficient R2 = 0.998), with a high sensitivity of 371 μA cm−2 mM−1, and a low detection limit of 500 nM (a signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1), which are comparable or superior to those of nonenzymatic H2O2 electrochemical sensors based on different nanohybrid electrode materials and even enzymatic H2O2 electrochemical sensors based on natural enzyme (Table S1), which reflects the improved sensing performances of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode for the highly sensitive detection of H2O2.

To investigate the selectivity of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF for H2O2 detection, the interference from endogenous electroactive substances such as glucose (GLU), uric acid (UA), ascorbic acid (AA) and dopamine (DA) on the response of H2O2 has been monitored by amperometric measurement at the applied potential of 0.05 V. Owing to the low overpotential of H2O2 reduction on AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode, no significant change occurs in the current response of 0.1 mM H2O2 after adding equal amount of interferent (Fig. 4f). Additionally, the interference from biothiols, including cysteine (Cys), cysteamine (CA), homocysteine (HCys), N-acetylcysteine (NAC), glutathione (GSH), 1,4-dithioerythritol(DTE), and dihydrolipoic acid (DHLA), which are abundant in biological systems, has further been evaluated. The results show that the changes of amperometric response of 0.1 mM H2O2 are less than 5% upon the addition of equal amount of biothiols in test solution (Table S2). These indicate the good anti-interference ability of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode. The reproducibility and repeatability of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode have been evaluated by detecting the amperometric responses of eight different microelectrodes to 20 μM H2O2 and six replicates of 20 μM H2O2 solution on one microelectrode, respectively (Fig. S8), the relative standard deviation (RSD) values are less than 4.8%, suggestive of the excellent reproducibility and repeatability of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode.

In this work, the proposed AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode has firstly been used in in-vitro electrochemical analysis of living breast cell samples, including breast cancer cell lines MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231, and human breast epithelial cell line HBL-100. Prior to it, the fluorescent calcein AM assay and standard cell counting Kit-8 (CCK-8) assay have been employed to test the cytotoxicity of the AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF to living cells. For fluorescent assay, MCF-7, MDA-MB-231 and HBL-100 cells were incubated with AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode for 5 h, followed by staining with calcein AM (AM = acetoxymethyl), a cell-permeant dye that commonly used in biology to evaluate the cell viability of most eukaryotic cells. This is due to the fact that calcein AM can be transported through the cellular membrane into cells, after that, the acetoxymethyl ester will hydrolyze by intracellular active esterases that only in living cells, and the nonfluorescent calcein AM convers to green-fluorescent (Karászi et al., 2001). As shown in the dark-field fluorescent image, most of these cells maintain vitality and give out strong green fluorescence ( Fig. 5a–c). Furthermore, the CCK-8 assay shows that MCF-7 cells maintain 98.7%, 97.3%, 95.2%, 90.9% and 87.4% viability after incubated with AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF for 1 h, 2 h, 3 h, 4 h and 5 h, respectively (Fig. 5d), which are 99.3%, 95.2%, 94.1%, 92.7%, 88.5% for MDA-MB-231 cells (Fig. 5e), and 99.5%, 96.2%, 95.6%, 93%, 90.4% for HBL-100 cells (Fig. 5f). The qualitative and quantitative analysis results collectively demonstrate the good biocompatibility of AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode to living cells.

For real-time tracking H2O2 released from different living human breast cells, the microelectrode was used as the working electrode and located near the cells by a micromanipulator in the test solution. To irritate living cells to release H2O2, N-formylmethionyl-leucyl-phenyl-alanine (fMLP) was selected as a stimulator. The results show that the addition of 50 µM fMLP in test solution containing 5.0 × 106 MCF-7 cells leads to an increased amperometric current density of 1.27 µA cm−2, which gradually decreases to background level upon the addition of catalase, an enzyme that can catalyze the breakdown of H2O2. For comparison, the addition of either fMLP or catalase in control solution without cells does not generate any changes in the amperometric curve (Fig. 5g). These indicate that the increased amperometric current response is originated from H2O2 secreted from MCF-7 cells. What's more, other two types of human breast cells, i.e., MDA-MB-231 and HBL-100, have been used as the target. The results show that under the same condition, the increased amperometric current densities are 1.01 µA cm−2 and 0.67 µA cm−2 for MDA-MB-231 and HBL-100, respectively (Fig. 5h), which is 79.5% and 52.8% of that for MCF-7 (Fig. 5i), revealing that the released amount of H2O2 varies from different types of cancer cells, and the human tumor cells generate more H2O2 than normal cells. This provides an effective strategy to identify different types of cancer cells.

Ultimately, the reliability and practicability of the proposed AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode have been verified by in situ detection of cancer biomarker in clinical breast cancer tissue. Fig. 6a shows the surgically resected clinical specimen from a female patient with primary breast cancer, where the breast cancer tissue was enclosed within adipose tissue. After detaching the adipose tissue, the breast cancer tissue was obtained (Fig. 6b). For the accurate and sensitive detection of H2O2 secreted from living cancer cells in breast cancer tissue, the flexible AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode was wound into a helical shape and inserting inside the breast cancer tissue (Fig. 6c), and the real-time amperometric response at AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode has been recorded. As shown in Fig. 6d, upon the addition of 50 µM fMLP in breast cancer tissue close to the microelectrode, a distinct increment of amperometric current density can be obsevered. When adding catalase, the amperometric current density gradually decreases to a background level. This should be due to the H2O2 secreted from living cancer cells in breast cancer tissue upon stimulation. Therefore, the electrochemical sensing platform based on AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode holds great promise for in-vitro and in-vivo detection of cancer biomarker in different clinical specimens for real application.

In summary, we develop a dual nanoenzyme modified microelectrode based on flexible CF coated with structurally integrated AuPd-ANPs decorated GQDs assembly, which is obtained by electrodepositing GQDs on substrate using ionic liquid electrolyte under a very negative potential to close-packed GODs assembly, followed by electrodepositing high-density ultrafine AuPd-ANPs on it. Benefiting from the unique structural merits of AuPd-ANPs and GQDs assembly and the synergistic effect of dual nanoenzymatic activities in H2O2 electrocatalytic reaction, the electrochemical sensing system based on flexible AuPd-ANPs/GQDs/ACF microelectrode exhibits good sensing performances, and can be used for real-time tracking H2O2 released from different types of living human breast cancer cells and in situ sensitive detection of H2O2 in clinical breast cancer tissue, which explores a new horizon in designing advanced implantable and wearable sensor devices for in-vivo and in-vitro electrochemical detection of different clinical specimens.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51572094 and 51504168) and Innovation Research Funds of HUST (No. 2017KFYXJJ164). We thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2018.02.026.
